svg.title: Wick Polynomials in Curved Spacetime via Hadamard Parametrix Subtraction
svg.desc: A schematic placeholder diagram for Wick polynomials on a curved spacetime, defined by point-splitting and Hadamard parametrix subtraction. Two spacetime points x and y are shown converging along a null geodesic on a curved cylinder representing a globally hyperbolic spacetime (M, g). The two-point function of the chosen Hadamard state and the geometry-determined Hadamard parametrix H(x, y) are subtracted at coincidence, leaving a smooth coincidence-limit value used to define the renormalised Wick power normal-ordered phi squared at x. The Hollands-Wald axioms (locality, covariance, scaling, Leibniz, smooth dependence on the metric) classify the remaining renormalisation freedom by a finite-dimensional family of local covariant counterterms; the renormalised stress-energy tensor is obtained from the same point-split subtraction applied to derivatives of the two-point function. No state-dependent choice survives the subtraction at leading order: the Hadamard parametrix is the same for every Hadamard state.
svg.text: Brunetti-Fredenhagen-Köhler 1996 / Hollands-Wald 2001: :φ²(x):_H from coincidence limit y → x.
Subtract the local Hadamard parametrix H(x, y) from the state two-point function ω₂(x, y); take y → x.
:φ²(x):_H := lim_{y → x} [ ω₂(x, y) − H(x, y) ]
H(x, y) = (8π²)⁻¹ U(x, y)/σ(x, y) + V(x, y) log σ(x, y) (local, state-independent)
Hollands-Wald axioms: locality, covariance, scaling, Leibniz, smooth metric dependence.
BFK 1996 Comm. Math. Phys. 180 633; Hollands-Wald 2001/2002/2003 Comm. Math. Phys. 223/231/237.
